config.title: Inequalities in AI use and exposure
config.abstract: Significant disparities exist in the use of AI between wealthy and poor nations due to limited internet, local language data, and digital skills. Addressing these gaps is essential to ensure low-income countries can participate in the AI-driven economy and prevent a widening global divide.
config.citation: Mahler, Daniel Gerszon, and Jan Willem Tulp. 2026. “Inequalities in AI Use, Foundations, and Exposure” In Atlas of  Global Development 2026, edited by A. F. Pirlea, U. Serajuddin, A. Thudt, D. Wadhwa, and M. Welch. Washington, DC: World Bank. License: Creative Commons Attribution CC BY 3.0 IGO.
config.credits: Daniel Gerszon Mahler, He Wang, Michael Weber
config.inputs: David Castillo Parra, Gabriel Demombynes, Holly Krambeck, Jorg Gero Langbein, Yan Liu, Sharada Srinivasan

content.text: The diffusion of artificial intelligence

Artificial intelligence (AI) is a rapidly spreading transformative technology. AI can be defined as a branch of computer science dedicated to creating systems capable of performing tasks that typically require human intelligence.[reference: worldbank2025] It has the potential to fundamentally shift how we learn, work, and live our daily lives. The unprecedented pace of AI adoption holds immense potential for accelerating global development and improving living standards. For example, it offers the potential of boosting productivity,[reference: bergman2025] personalizing education,[reference: molina2024] and transforming the agricultural sector.[reference: krishnakumari2025] However, the diffusion of this powerful technology is far from even.

Measuring the use of AI is challenging, in part due to the many ways one can interact with AI and the speed with which this is changing. High-frequency website traffic data reveals a great divergence in the use of AI across countries. This is in part due to greater internet penetration in wealthier nations, but AI use per internet user is also much greater in wealthier nations. Focusing on the most used AI platform, ChatGPT, as of May 2025, traffic per internet user in high-income countries was nearly 50 times that of low-income countries.[reference: liu2025][reference: semrush2025]
Traffic to ChatGPT per internet user is [emphasis: 50 times] greater in high-income countries than in low-income countries, as of May 2025.[reference: liu2025][reference: semrush2025]

content.title: Internet users in rich countries use ChatGPT 50 times more than in poorer countries
content.subtitle: Traffic to ChatGPT per internet user, May 2025
content.source: Liu, Huang, and Wang (2025), sourced from Semrush.
content.prompt_text: Where in the world are people using AI?
content.keyFactLabel: more ChatGPT usage occurs per internet user in high-income countries than in low-income countries.

content.text: This discrepancy is in part due to the use of different chatbots around the world. For example, ChatGPT is relatively rarely used in China where other chatbots dominate. Yet even measures that try to account for all chatbots find that in most countries in Sub-Saharan Africa, less than 10% of the working-age population is using AI.[reference: misra2025] Evidence from Anthropic likewise suggests that country-usage is lowest in poorer countries, with usage scores more than 10 times greater in high-income countries than in low-income countries.[reference: anthropic2026]
Barriers to AI adoption

The disparity in AI adoption is not accidental, it is rooted in difficult development challenges. The Digital Progress and Trends Report 2025 identifies core foundations necessary for countries to harness the benefits of AI, including connectivity, context, competency, and computing power. Connectivity refers to having access to reliable internet; context to the availability of relevant, local data that makes AI tools useful and accurate; and competency to the presence of a digitally skilled population that can build, adapt, and effectively use AI technologies. Computing power refers to specialized AI chips, servers, storage, and data centers, and forms the physical backbone supporting AI. In many low- and middle-income countries, these foundations are weak, creating significant barriers to AI use. Without these elements in place, the promise of AI remains out of reach for a significant portion of the world’s population.

[emphasis: Connectivity and affordability: ] One key reason for lack of AI use in low- and middle-income countries is lack of internet access.[reference: pirlea2026] Yet AI use is lower in poorer countries even when only considering the population that uses the internet. This is partly because data is prohibitively costly for many in poorer countries. For AI applications to be useful, they require consistent and significant data consumption. Yet in low-income countries, the price of a data-only 5GB mobile package makes up more than 15% of the average person's consumption. In these countries, around 60% of consumption is allocated to food.[reference: jolliffe2025] This means that a data package requires nearly half of the budget left after satisfying nutritional needs.
In low-income countries, a data-only 5GB mobile package takes up[emphasis:  nearly half] of the household budget that remains after food consumption.

As a result, many households in low-income countries would need to hold back on spending such as health care, education, and clothing to afford a 5 GB data package. This affordability gap locks out hundreds of millions from using AI, creating a two-tiered system where those with the financial means can access the tools that drive productivity and opportunity, while those without are left further behind.

content.title: Mobile data is prohibitively expensive in low-income countries
content.subtitle: Cost of data-only 5GB mobile package as a share of mean consumption
content.source: International Telecommunication Union (ITU), World Bank’s Poverty and Inequality Platform (PIP), Jolliffe et al. (2025), and U.S. Department of Agriculture Economic Research Service.
content.prompt_text: Can everyone afford to use AI?

content.text: [emphasis: ]

[emphasis: Context: The Digital Language Divide.] Large language models, which AI relies upon, require vast amounts of training data reflecting local languages and realities, such as text, images, and cultural information. The digital world is overwhelmingly dominated by English, and it is estimated that English accounts for[emphasis:  ]nearly half of all global URLs and an even greater share of the high-quality text data used to train leading AI models. This creates a massive gap in training data for the majority of the world's population who do not speak English as their first language. As a result, AI models often lack the nuance and accuracy needed to be useful in non-English contexts.[reference: solatorio2024] In 2023 it was estimated that 4 billion people worldwide speak a language not served by chatbots.[reference: myers2023]

content.label: worldwide speak a language not served well by chatbots.[reference: myers2023]

content.text: This linguistic imbalance is a critical barrier, as it limits the utility of current AI tools while perpetuating a digital world where only a fraction of global cultures and languages are represented.

content.title: Languages spoken in poorer countries are underrepresented in AI training data
content.subtitle: Global URLs and global population, by language
content.source: Common Crawl, LinguaMeta, and Solatorio et al. (2024) (based on data from Etnologue).
content.prompt_text: Are AI tools equally useful and accurate in all languages?
content.keyFactLabel: worldwide speak a language not served well by chatbots
content.xAxisLabel: Share of global population speaking language (log)

content.text: [emphasis: ]
[emphasis: ]
[emphasis: ]

[emphasis: Competency: The Human Capital Gap. ]A third barrier is the global deficit in digital skills. Using AI effectively requires digital literacy and for certain applications advanced technical skills. In many low- and middle-income countries, these skills are in critically short supply. In 2023, it was estimated that less than 5% of the population in low-income countries possessed basic digital skills.

content.label: in low-income countries had basic digital skills in 2023.[reference: ITU]

content.text: The gap is even more severe for advanced skills needed to build and maintain AI systems. Without a concerted effort to build digital skills at all levels of the education system and to retain local talent, poorer countries will be unable to move from being passive consumers of AI to active participants and creators in the AI economy.

content.title: Most people in low-income countries lack the digital skills to use AI
content.subtitle: Population with digital skills, 2023
content.source: World Bank (2025), based on data from the International Telecommunication Union (ITU).
content.prompt_text: Does everyone have the skills to use AI?

content.text: [emphasis: ]

[emphasis: Computing power.] A fourth barrier is the lack of computing power, AI chips and servers in many poorer countries. As of June 2025, high-income countries accounted for 77 percent of global data center capacity and low-income countries less than 0.1 percent.[reference: worldbank2025] On a per capita basis, the US has 20,000 times as many servers as low-income countries.[reference: worldbank2025] High entry costs, lack of energy, and digital infrastructure constraints often deter private investment in poorer countries. Yet computing power is highly tradable across countries. With access from other nations, poor countries can use AI even without having national computing power.
Exposure to AI at work

There is a direct link between this lack of usage of AI in low- and middle-income countries and lack of exposure to AI at work.[reference: dem2026] This matters, because exposure to AI means that some tasks performed can be augmented by AI, which can increase workers’ productivity, while other tasks may be automated, possibly impacting demand for labor. Whichever it is, high exposure suggests that the nature of these jobs is likely to change.

Occupational exposure to AI can be measured by the AI Occupational Exposure index, which links specific human job tasks to various artificial intelligence applications.[reference: felten2021] It uses a detailed database of occupations to determine how much of a person's daily work duties overlap with current AI capabilities. By analyzing these connections, the index identifies which jobs are most likely to be affected or assisted by the emerging technology. The scale ranges from 0 to 100, where 0 represents an occupation with no tasks suitable for AI and 100 indicates a role where every task is fully exposed to AI capabilities. Actual usage data validates these theoretical exposure indices, showing that adoption patterns closely track where jobs are most susceptible to AI’s influence.[reference: dem2026][footnote: Yet the link between use and exposure is not perfect. Managers, for example, show high theoretical exposure to AI but relatively low usage.]

content.title: Workers in low-income countries are less exposed to AI
content.subtitle: Index of occupational exposure to AI from 0 (no exposure) to 100 (full exposure)
content.source: Author's calculations based on Demombynes, Langbein, and Weber (2025). The occupational icons are AI generated.
content.keyFactLabel: of workers in low-income countries are highly exposed to AI.

scenes.text: Some occupations, such as Information and Communications Technology Professionals, are exposed to AI in most facets of their work, with an exposure score of 93.
Others, such as Subsistence Farmers, Fishers, Hunters and Gatherers, are largely not exposed by AI in their work, with an exposure score of 20.
Most occupations fall somewhere in between.

Countries are exposed differently to AI because occupation types differ by country. We can see this by scaling the occupations according to how prominent they are. Here this is done for the United States. Defining high exposure as greater than 50, about half of all workers in the US are highly exposed, because occupations such as Business and Administration Associate Professionals and Teaching Professionals are common.

In low-income countries, to the contrary, where large populations of workers are engaged in farming, less than 20% of workers are highly exposed to AI.[footnote: These statistics depend on where one sets the threshold for high exposure. Regardless of how it is defined, workers in low-income countries are notably less exposed to AI than workers in high-income countries.]

content.text: The difference between exposure in poor and rich countries is likely underestimated for two reasons: Firstly, many in poorer countries lack internet and and computer at home or at work. Therefore, even if their occupational tasks could be exposed to AI, in practice, they are not[reference: gmyrek2024]. This highlights how foundational barriers prevent workers in poorer nations from utilizing AI, even when their roles are otherwise ready for it. Secondly, the tasks associated with an occupation differ in rich and poor countries. The AI Occupational Exposure Index is based on the tasks associated with occupations in the US. In poorer countries, similar occupations likely have tasks with less AI exposure. In sum, these two reasons can explain why high-income countries use AI about 4 times as much as middle-income countries, while their gap in the AI Occupational Exposure Index is notably less.

AI exposure also varies notably within countries. Exposure is heavily concentrated among urban populations, with city dwellers facing much higher exposure than those in rural areas due to the nature of urban employment. Education is the strongest predictor of exposure, as individuals with post-secondary degrees occupy roles with high cognitive task intensity that are most susceptible to AI integration. A notable gender gap exists, with women generally experiencing higher exposure than men, largely because they are more concentrated in clerical, professional, and service roles.[reference: dem2025] Understanding these differences in exposure is vital to anticipate how AI will transform the labor market, and the socio-economic consequences of this  transformation.

In middle-income countries, usage is highly concentrated among a few professions; ICT workers and teachers account for nearly three-quarters of all AI usage, compared to a much broader spread across sectors in high-income countries. This concentration reinforces the impact of foundational barriers, as adoption remains restricted to workers with the strongest digital access and text-heavy tasks.[reference: dem2026]
Navigating the path forward

The rise of AI offers a powerful engine for productivity and human development, yet it also threatens to deepen existing inequalities between and within countries. The impact of AI on jobs remains a subject of intense debate, with the future likely to involve a complex mix of both augmentation, where AI enhances human capabilities, and automation, where AI replaces certain tasks. As AI adoption follows a predictable diffusion pattern—starting with ICT professionals and spreading outward to other sectors—it offers a window for proactive policy. Until now, the direct impact in the poorest countries has been limited by structural barriers and the predominant occupations workers have.

For low- and middle-income countries, it is vital to strengthen the foundational pillars of connectivity, context, and competency, as well as computing power. This means investing in affordable internet and data center infrastructure, fostering the creation of local and multilingual data, and embarking on a massive effort to build digital skills throughout the population, starting with those sectors closest to the initial wave of adoption.
